title: Apply: The Fourteenth Amendment—An Overview
description: Apply your understanding of the the Fourteenth Amendment with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: The Fourteenth Amendment: Citizenship, Freedom, and Equality

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Ohio Representative John Bingham’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866.**

I propose, with the help of this Congress and of the American people, that hereafter there shall not be any disregard of that essential guarantee of your Constitution in any State of the Union. And how? By simply adding an amendment to the Constitution to operate on Congress the power to pass all laws necessary and proper to secure all persons—which includes every citizen of every State—their equal personal rights; and if the [[☃ definition 1]] of South Carolina will not respect the rights of the citizens of Massachusetts under the Constitution of their common country, I desire to see the Federal judiciary clothed with the power to [[☃ definition 2]] this question, and assert those rights by solemn judgment, inflicting upon the offenders such penalties as will [[☃ definition 3]] a decent respect for this guarantee to all the citizens of every State. 

**What is Bingham proposing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An amendment that would give Congress the power to pass laws to ensure equal rights for all citizens in every state
An amendment that would allow Congress to replace tribunals in states that do not protect individual rights
An amendment that would detail how former confederate rebels would be punished following the end of the Civil War
An amendment that would allow the states to ignore federal laws they believe are not necessary and proper

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Ohio Representative John Bingham’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866.**

I propose, with the help of this Congress and of the American people, that hereafter there shall not be any disregard of that essential guarantee of your Constitution in any State of the Union. And how? By simply adding an amendment to the Constitution to operate on Congress the power to pass all laws necessary and proper to secure all persons—which includes every citizen of every State—their equal personal rights; and if the [[☃ definition 1]] of South Carolina will not respect the rights of the citizens of Massachusetts under the Constitution of their common country, I desire to see the Federal judiciary clothed with the power to [[☃ definition 2]] this question, and assert those rights by solemn judgment, inflicting upon the offenders such penalties as will [[☃ definition 3]] a decent respect for this guarantee to all the citizens of every State. 

**The change that Bingham proposes would give Congress the authority to take which action?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To admit former Confederate states into the Union
To allow every citizen to introduce constitutional amendments
To replace judges in the federal courts who do not uphold equal rights
To require states to protect the rights of citizens

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Ohio Representative John Bingham’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866.**

Every slave the moment he is [[☃ definition 1]] becomes a “free citizen,” in the words of the [[☃ definition 2]], becomes a “free person,” which embraces all citizens, in the words of our Constitution, becomes equal before the law with every other citizen of the United States. 

What, then, sir, is the result? Simply this, that the Constitution must be amended! I agree, if the [[☃ definition 3]] rebel States would make no denial of right to the emancipated citizens no amendment would be needed. But they will make denial. 

**According to Bingham, what is the goal of the amendment he’s proposing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the alliance of the states that make up the United States

choices.content: To end slavery and free enslaved people
To give formerly enslaved people the rights of equal citizenship
To define the naturalization process
To punish the former Confederacy

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Ohio Representative John Bingham’s comments in a session of the 39th Congress in 1866.**

Every slave the moment he is [[☃ definition 1]] becomes a “free citizen,” in the words of the [[☃ definition 2]], becomes a “free person,” which embraces all citizens, in the words of our Constitution, becomes equal before the law with every other citizen of the United States. 

What, then, sir, is the result? Simply this, that the Constitution must be amended! I agree, if the [[☃ definition 3]] rebel States would make no denial of right to the emancipated citizens no amendment would be needed. But they will make denial. 

**According to Bingham, why is the amendment he proposes necessary?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the alliance of the states that make up the United States

choices.content: Former Confederate states have shown that they will follow the Constitution and protect the rights of emancipated citizens.
Formerly enslaved people are not citizens of the United States and not entitled to the rights and protections of citizenship.
Former Confederate states will not give formerly enslaved people the rights they are entitled to as citizens of the United States.
Formerly enslaved people have petitioned Congress to ensure they are equal before the law with all other citizens.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a footnote in the majority decision in *United States v. Carolene Products Company* (1938).**

[P]rejudice against [[☃ definition 1]] minorities may be a special condition, which tends seriously to [[☃ definition 2]] the operation of those political processes ordinarily be relied upon to protect minorities, and which may call for a correspondingly more [[☃ definition 3]] judicial inquiry. 

**Which statement best summarizes the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: separate and isolated from other people

choices.content: Judges who feel prejudice toward minorities should not be involved in cases relating to the rights of those minorities.
Judges may allow laws that specifically target minority groups, if the laws are important to the operations of the government.
Judges may need to look more closely at laws that target minorities, as prejudice can make it difficult for minorities to rely on other types of protection.
Judges have no authority review laws that they believe unfairly target minority groups.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a footnote in the majority decision in *United States v. Carolene Products Company* (1938).**

[P]rejudice against [[☃ definition 1]] may be a special condition, which tends seriously to [[☃ definition 2]] the operation of those political processes ordinarily be relied upon to protect minorities, and which may call for a correspondingly more [[☃ definition 3]] judicial inquiry. 

**This famous footnote eventually led to the establishment of which standard?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: separate and isolated from other people

choices.content: strict scrutiny
the *Lemon* test
judicial review
rational basis review

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Pennsylvania representative Thaddeus Stevens’s comments proposing the Fourteenth Amendment in the House of Representatives in 1866.**

. . . I can hardly believe that any person can be found who will not admit that every one of these provisions is just. They are all asserted, in some form or other, in our DECLARATION or [[☃ definition 3]] law. But the Constitution limits only the action of Congress, and is not a limitation on the States. This amendment [[☃ definition 1]] that defect, and allows Congress to correct the unjust legislation of the States, so far that the law which operates upon one man shall operate equally upon all. . . . Now, different degrees of punishment are inflicted, not on account of the magnitude of the crime, but according to the color of the skin. Now, color disqualifies a man from testifying in courts, or being tried in the same way as white men. I need not [[☃ definition 2]] these partial and oppressive laws. Unless the Constitution should restrain them those States will all, I fear, keep up this discrimination, and crush to death the hated freedmen. . . .

**According to Stevens, what problem with the Constitution would the Fourteenth Amendment correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution limits the powers of state governments and leaves them at the mercy of the federal government.
The Constitution does not give sentencing guidelines for many crimes, leaving that power up to the states.
The Constitution does not give the Supreme Court the power to strike down federal laws that unfairly target Black men.
The Constitution places limits on the federal government, but does not set important limits on the powers of the states.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Pennsylvania representative Thaddeus Stevens’s comments proposing the Fourteenth Amendment in the House of Representatives in 1866.**

. . . I can hardly believe that any person can be found who will not admit that every one of these provisions is just. They are all asserted, in some form or other, in our DECLARATION or [[☃ definition 1]] law. But the Constitution limits only the action of Congress, and is not a limitation on the States. This amendment [[☃ definition 2]] that defect, and allows Congress to correct the unjust legislation of the States, so far that the law which operates upon one man shall operate equally upon all. . . . Now, different degrees of punishment are inflicted, not on account of the magnitude of the crime, but according to the color of the skin. Now, color disqualifies a man from testifying in courts, or being tried in the same way as white men. I need not [[☃ definition 3]] these partial and oppressive laws. Unless the Constitution should restrain them those States will all, I fear, keep up this discrimination, and crush to death the hated freedmen. . . .

**Which development best supports Stevens’s argument that “different degrees of punishment are inflicted . . . according to the color of the skin”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The ratification of the Thirteenth Amendment
The implementation of the Black Codes
The creation of the rational-basis test
The use of literacy tests as a requirement to vote

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Jacob Howard’s 1866 comments to the Senate about the proposed Fourteenth Amendment.**

[Section 5] casts upon Congress the responsibility of seeing to it, for the future, that all the sections of the amendment are carried out in good faith, and that no State infringes the rights of persons or property . . . It enables Congress, in case the States shall enact laws in conflict with the principles of the amendment, to correct that legislation by a formal congressional [[☃ definition 1]].

**According to the excerpt, which specific power does Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment grant?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the act of making a law official

choices.content: Citizenship given by birthright
Equality for all people
Freedoms guaranteed by the Bill of Rights
National power over civil rights

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Jacob Howard’s 1866 comments to the Senate about the proposed Fourteenth Amendment.**

[Section 5] casts upon Congress the responsibility of seeing to it, for the future, that all the sections of the amendment are carried out in good faith, and that no State infringes the rights of persons or property . . . It enables Congress, in case the States shall enact laws in conflict with the principles of the amendment, to correct that legislation by a formal congressional [[☃ definition 1]].

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the act of making a law official

choices.content: The Fourteenth Amendment requires states to seek Congressional approval before enacting any laws related to individual rights or property.
The Fourteenth Amendment immediately invalidated all existing state laws that were inconsistent with the principles of the amendment upon its ratification.
The Fourteenth Amendment gives the federal government power to pass laws to prevent states from violating the principles outlined in the amendment.
The Fourteenth Amendment gave the federal government supreme authority over state governments on all matters.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Pennsylvania representative Thaddeus Stevens’s comments on the proposed Fourteenth Amendment in the House of Representatives in 1866.**

Unless the Constitution should restrain them, those States will all, I fear, keep up this discrimination and crush to death the hated freedmen. Some answer, “Your [[☃ definition 1]] secures the same things”. That is partly true, but a law is [[☃ definition 2]] by a majority. And I need hardly say that the first time that the South with their [[☃ definition 3]] allies obtain the command of Congress it will be repealed. The veto of the President and their votes on the bill are [[☃ definition 4]] evidence of that. And yet I am amazed and alarmed at the impatience of certain well-meaning Republicans at the exclusion of the rebel States until the Constitution shall be so amended as to restrain their [[☃ definition 5]] desires. This amendment once adopted cannot be [[☃ definition 6]] without two thirds of Congress. That they will hardly get.

**According to Stevens, why was the Fourteenth Amendment necessary?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: Northerners who opposed the Civil War and wanted to peacefully settle with the Confederates
tyrannical; exercising power in cruel ways

choices.content: It was the only method the federal government had to restrict state laws.
It could not be repealed or stopped as easily as laws could.
It was easier to pass an amendment than a federal law.
It did not need any support from Southern states to pass.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Pennsylvania representative Thaddeus Stevens’s comments on the proposed Fourteenth Amendment in the House of Representatives in 1866.**

Unless the Constitution should restrain them, those States will all, I fear, keep up this discrimination and crush to death the hated freedmen. Some answer, “Your [[☃ definition 1]] secures the same things”. That is partly true, but a law is [[☃ definition 2]] by a majority. And I need hardly say that the first time that the South with their [[☃ definition 3]] allies obtain the command of Congress it will be repealed. The veto of the President and their votes on the bill are [[☃ definition 4]] evidence of that. And yet I am amazed and alarmed at the impatience of certain well-meaning Republicans at the exclusion of the rebel States until the Constitution shall be so amended as to restrain their [[☃ definition 5]] desires. This amendment once adopted cannot be [[☃ definition 6]] without two thirds of Congress. That they will hardly get.

**Stevens is referring to which development when he says, “States will all, I fear, keep up this discrimination”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: Northerners who opposed the Civil War and wanted to peacefully settle with the Confederates
tyrannical; exercising power in cruel ways

choices.content: The passage of the Black Codes to limit the rights of free Black people
The implementation of Jim Crow laws to segregate people by race
The forced relocation of all formerly enslaved people to northern states
The use of racial qualifications for candidates for public office
